coredata.dc:title: Chemical synthesis of carbon dots with blue, green and red emission for dopamine reversible switching probes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaotong
Zhao, Qingling
Song, Qinghong
Bu, He
Gao, Jie
Li, Lanlan
Yu, Xiaofei
Yang, Xiaojing
Lu, Zunming
Zhang, Xinghua
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) possess the merits such as energy efficiency, green sustainability and environmental friendliness, comparing with top-down synthesis methods at higher pressure or temperature condition. Here, a variety of emission states CDs were prepared by using the method of room temperature chemistry by selecting green raw materials such as glucose, p-phthalaldehyde and m-diethylaminophenol. The luminescence mechanism was studied in detail. The luminescent center of blue emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) and green emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) is CO bond, and the increased contents of CO bond lead to the creation of new energy levels between the energy gaps of HOMO and LUMO levels, which results in the red shift of luminescence wavelength. The emission state of red emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) is due to the formation of amino N. In addition, R-CDs have an exclusive respond to dopamine (DA) and are regarded as good fluorescent probes for detecting DA. Furthermore, the addition of ascorbic acid (AA) restores the luminescence of R-CDs quenched by DA. Therefore, R-CDs has great application potential as a selective fluorescent “turn on-off” probe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chemical synthesis
Photoluminescence
Dopamine detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs
Chemical synthesis and morphology of prepared CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52271210, 22179032, 52171176), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. E2021202022, B2021202011), and Tianjin Health Research Project (No. ZC20112).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of chemical synthesis for CDs, TEM images and size distribution (inset) of (b) B-CDs, (c) G-CDs, and (d) R-CDs, respectively.
(a) FTIR spectra, (b) UV–Vis spectra, (c) emission spectra, and (d) fluorescence decay curves of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively.
(a) Full scan of XPS spectra, (b) C 1s and (c) O 1s spectra and fitting curves of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs, (d) N 1s spectrum and fitting results of R-CDs.
Fluorescence mechanism diagram of multicolor fluorescent CDs.

(a) Selective detection of DA by R-CDs, (b) emission spectra of R-CDs with different concentrations of DA (0–300 μM), inset: the linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and [DA], (c) fluorescence decay curves of R-CDs with various concentrations of DA (0–160 μM), and (d) Stern-Volmer plots under different temperatures (293 K, 298 and 303 K) and various concentrations of DA (0–80 μM).
(a) Emission spectra, (b) excitation spectra, (c) absorption spectra and (d) decay curves of R-CDs and DA-R-CDs solution with various concentrations of AA (0–10 mg/mL), respectively.
Emission peaks and QY of typical CDs prepared with chemical methods.
Experimental results of standard addition recovery rate of DA in tablets.

head.ce:title: Chemical synthesis of carbon dots with blue, green and red emission for dopamine reversible switching probes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tian Jin Medical Union Center, Tianjin 300122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tian Jin Medical Union Center, Tianjin 300122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) possess the merits such as energy efficiency, green sustainability and environmental friendliness, comparing with top-down synthesis methods at higher pressure or temperature condition. Here, a variety of emission states CDs were prepared by using the method of room temperature chemistry by selecting green raw materials such as glucose, p-phthalaldehyde and m-diethylaminophenol. The luminescence mechanism was studied in detail. The luminescent center of blue emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) and green emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) is CO bond, and the increased contents of CO bond lead to the creation of new energy levels between the energy gaps of HOMO and LUMO levels, which results in the red shift of luminescence wavelength. The emission state of red emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) is due to the formation of amino N. In addition, R-CDs have an exclusive respond to dopamine (DA) and are regarded as good fluorescent probes for detecting DA. Furthermore, the addition of ascorbic acid (AA) restores the luminescence of R-CDs quenched by DA. Therefore, R-CDs has great application potential as a selective fluorescent “turn on-off” probe.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new kind of zero-dimensional nanomaterials, CDs are mainly consists of sp2/sp3 carbon and surface functional groups containing oxygen/nitrogen elements [1–3]. Due to the stable photochemical properties, excellent color rendering ability, good biocompatibility and electron transport ability [4–6], CDs provide obvious advantages in the fields of ion detection [7], cryptographic anti-counterfeiting [8], medical sensing [9] and bio-imaging [10]. Since the first discovery in 2004, CDs have attracted widespread research interest [11]. The synthetic routes of carbon dots are mainly consist of “top-down methods” [12] and “bottom-up methods” [13]. The bottom-up methods are the most common synthesis methods of CDs, which are conducive to the preparation of CDs with bright fluorescence [14,15].

In the bottom-up approaches, the chemical synthesis of CDs has the benefits of mild reaction condition, simple operation and easy batch preparation. Therefore, the chemical synthesis of CDs at room temperature is receiving more attentions. The reaction mechanism of chemical methods includes acid-base neutralization, aldol condensation, concentrated acid oxidation and schiff base reaction, etc. [16–19]. Hou et al. [16] inadvertently mixed acetone and NaOH without any treatment, and it was found that the mixture was converted into brown solid product of CDs after several days of storage. Wei et al. [17] dissolved glucose in ethylenediamine by neutralization reaction, then added phosphoric acid to the mixture by violent stirring to generate reaction heat, and the obtained yellow-brown supernatant was N-doped CDs. In addition, Zhang et al. obtained green luminescent CDs by using only sulfuric acid to control sucrose carbonization without surface passivation [18]. Among them, the currently selected raw materials for aldol condensation reaction are mainly used toxic reagents such as acetone and acetaldehyde, which needs a long time to react. In addition, the emission wavelength of CDs prepared by chemical methods is mostly concentrated in the blue and green regions (450–560 nm), and only few report of red emitting CDs [20]. At present, there is a certain difficulty in achieving red light emission, and specific groups need to be introduced to achieve it [21,22]. It is well known that the progress of chemical reactions at room temperature is subject to certain limitation, and even no reaction occurs. Since the realization of red fluorescence requires a narrower energy gap, non-radiative transitions are more likely to occur than other short-wavelength luminescence, resulting in fluorescence quenching. However, the surface groups of CDs synthesized by chemical method are numerous, and the collision between groups leads to non-radiative recombination of energy, indicating that red fluorescence is more difficult to occur. Therefore, the formation of a stable single dominant luminescent center is the key to achieve red emission in chemical methods. A series of side reactions such as carbonization, dehydration, and cross-linking in the chemical reactions result in a high concentration of surface defects and serious non-radiative transitions for CDs, which lead to the low efficiency of chemical method. Therefore, using green and non-toxic raw materials with specific groups which can react at room temperature is the key to achieve red light emission for chemical synthesis of CDs.

We chose non-toxic sugars containing hydroxyl groups and green organic molecules containing aldehyde groups as raw materials to modulate the hydroxyl-aldehyde condensation reaction process and improve the emission wavelength and efficiency of chemically synthesized CDs. During the reaction process, m-diethylaminophenol was added to achieve wavelength regulation of R-CDs. The quantum yield (QY) for chemical synthesis of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs is 0.88%, 7.43%, and 1.42%, respectively. The photoluminescence process of polychromatic luminescent CDs is also elucidated. The emission of B-CDs and G-CDs is primarily induced by the CO bonds. With the increased contents of CO bonds, the emission peak redshifts from blue to green emission. Since m-diethylaminophenol contains benzene ring with specific groups, the hydroxyl and aldehyde groups react with diethylamine groups, making amino N responsible for the red emission of R-CDs. In addition, R-CDs have good selectivity for dopamine (DA) detection. The fluorescence quenching of R-CDs displayed a favorable linear correlation with the concentration of DA in the range of 0–140 μM. The minimum detection limit of DA is 0.49 μM, and DA in real tablets can be also detected with high accuracy and repeatability. Moreover, the quenched fluorescence of R-CDs can be restored with ascorbic acid (AA), and the R-CDs have great potential as a selective fluorescent “turn on-off” probe for DA.

Glucose was procured from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Factory. p-Phthalaldehyde and m-diethylaminophenol were purchased from Kmart (Tianjin) Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. NaOH was bought from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. The dialysis bag (500 Da) was acquired from Beijing Yikang Shengshi Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Dopamine was obtained from Aladdin Company. Ascorbic acid was attained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The deionized water was used in the experiment. The levodopa tablets were acquired from Beijing Shuguang Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.25 g glucose used as carbon source and 0.5 g NaOH as catalyst were dispersed in 5 mL H2O. The compound was stirred magnetically for 3 h and placed under ambient air. After 4 days, H2O was added to dissolve the solution, which was further purified by water dialysis with a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 500), and finally B-CDs were obtained. In addition, G-CDs were obtained with 0.25 g glucose, 0.5 g p-phthalaldehyde and 0.5 g NaOH in the same way. Moreover, using 0.25 g glucose, 0.5 g p-phthalaldehyde and 0.1 g m-diethylaminophenol as the carbon sources of R-CDs, and R-CDs were synthsized with the identical process.

The 1 mL R-CDs were mixed with 3 mL fresh DA aqueous solution with different concentrations at room temperature, and the final concentration range of DA was 0–300 μM. The fluorescence was measured by a spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 540 nm after one hour. The calibration curve was established by using the fluorescence intensity of the maximum emission wavelength to calculate the concentration of DA in the actual sample.

Morphology of CDs was performed on a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20) at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analyses were carried out using a ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer with a mono Al Kα X-ray source. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were performed on a spectrometer (Bruker, WQF-410) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The fluorescence spectra, decay curves and quantum yield of the obtained CDs were acquired turough a steady and transient state spectrometer (Horiba, FL-3-22). The ultraviolet visible absorption spectra (UV–Vis) were recorded with a spectrophotometer (Hitachi, U-3900H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical synthesis and morphology of prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve chemical preparation of CDs with various fluorescence emission, non-toxic raw materials containing specific hydroxyl and aldehyde groups were used to synthesize B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs (Fig. S1). Fig. 1 a shows the synthetic mechanism of three CDs. Under the action of NaOH catalyst, the α-C of glucose is dehydrogenated to form enol ester, and nucleophilic addition reaction occurs with the carbonyl groups of another glucose. The addition product takes a proton from the solvent to form β hydroxyl aldehyde. These products undergo polymerization reaction to form polymers with protracted carbon chains, which eventually get through curling, winding, or carbonization crosslinking to form B-CDs. With addition of p-phthalaldehdye, its carbonyl group undergoes a nucleophilic addition reaction with the α-C of glucose to form G-CDs. For R-CDs, m-diethylaminophenol was introduced into the process of glucose and p-phthalaldehyde to cause a series of reactions between hydroxyl, aldehyde and diethylamine groups, successfully introducing the N atoms, thus forming R-CDs.
Furthermore, the reaction under such strong alkali conditions generates heat and increases the temperature, which will produce some side reactions such as dehydration and hydroxylation.

The morphological structure and diameter distribution of three CDs using TEM is displayed in Fig. 1b-c. The prepared CDs exhibit spherical morphology and have good water dispersibility. The average diameters of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs are 1.03 ± 0.16 nm, 1.13 ± 0.23 nm, and 1.39 ± 0.34 nm, respectively. The main reason for the different photoluminescence properties of the three CDs is not induced by the quantum size effect because of the similar size. In addition, the HRTEM images of the three CDs (Fig. S2) show that there are unclear lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite, indicating the prepared CDs have low crystallinity.

The chemical bonds of CDs are analyzed by FTIR spectra. In Fig. 2 a, it shows that the three CDs contain the similar chemical bonds, mainly including OH bond at 3330 cm−1, CH bond at 2930, 821 and 740 cm−1, CO bond at 1595 cm−1, CC bond at 1442 cm−1 and CO bond at 1049 cm−1 [23]. The difference between R-CDs and the other two kinds of CDs is that R-CDs have chemical bonds associated with N, incorporating NH2 at 3365 cm−1 and CN bond at 1352 cm−1 [24]. As a result, the different fluorescence characteristics between B-CDs and G-CDs are mainly originated from relative contents of chemical bonds, while the introduction of the N related chemical bonds will induce the luminescent properties of R-CDs. The absorption peak intensity of CO and OH bonds of G-CDs are higher than that of B-CDs. The fluorescence of R-CDs is due to the reaction between diethylamine and hydroxyl groups to form amino groups, which lead to the red emission. In addition, we have also tried other N sources (urea, dicyandiamide and ethylenediamine) to synthesize CDs (Fig. S3), but the emission peaks of prepared CDs are not situated in red light. This is because m-diethylaminophenol contains aromatic ring, and the lone pair electrons on the nitrogen will pair with the π electrons on the aromatic ring, thus reducing the electron density of the nitrogen, and the CN bonds become easier to break and react. Consequently, compared with small molecules, it is more easily to be synthesized long wavelength emitting CDs [25,26].

The spectral characteristics of three CDs are investigated by absorption and luminescence spectra. Fig. 2b displays that both B-CDs and G-CDs show π-π* electron transition with strong absorption peaks centered at 230 and 268 nm, respectively, which are originated from the CC bonding of CDs [27]. The absorption peaks at 200–300 nm and 375 nm for R-CDs are induced by the n-π* electron transition of CO/CN bonds [28]. The luminescence properties of prepared CDs are generally originated from quantum size effects and surface states. The different electronic transitions of the three CDs indicate that the fluorescence spectra are mainly caused by surface defects. Fig. 2c displays that the luminescence peaks are located at 451 nm, 523 nm, 598 nm for B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. When only glucose is used as the carbon source, the emission peak location of B-CDs is nearly constant as the content of glucose (Fig. S5a). The green emission intensity increases with the p-phthalaldehyde and reaches the maximum as p-phthalaldehyde is added with 0.5 g (Fig. S5b), demonstrating that the increase of hydroxyl content after the aldol condensation reaction affects the green light emission. The addition of m-diethylaminophenol for R-CDs leads to the red shift of emission peak to 598 nm. The optimal performance of R-CDs can be achieved as m-ethylaminophenol is used with 90 mg (Fig. S5c), suggesting that the amino-induced generation of m-diethylaminophenol drastically changes the emission state of CDs.

Fig. 2d displays the fluorescence decay curves of three types of CDs, and the average lifetimes of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs are 6.01 ns, 20.65 ns, and 6.22 ns, respectively. It is presumed that the long lifetime of G-CDs is resulted from that the surface of G-CDs contains more OH groups, and the residence time of excited electrons becomes longer, allowing more electrons to return to the ground states through radiation transition [34,35]. Table 1 provides the emission wavelength and quantum yields of typical CDs prepared with chemical methods. Here, the QY of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs is 0.88%, 7.43% and 1.42%, respectively. The radiation rate constants (KR) and the non-radiation rate constants (KnR) of CDs are calculated in accordance with the following formula:
(1)
K R
= Q Y / τ
(2)
K
nR
=
( 1 - Q Y )
/ τ
The KR of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs is 1.47 × 106 s−1, 3.60 × 106 s−1 and 2.28 × 106 s−1, respectively, and the corresponded KnR is 1.65 × 108 s−1, 4.48 × 107 s−1 and 1.59 × 108 s−1, respectively (Table S3). G-CDs have the highest QY and the lowest KnR, indicating that the OH functional groups of G-CDs enhance π electron density and suppress non-radiative decay processes [36]. The QY of R-CDs is slightly higher than that of B-CDs, which indicates that the electrons trapped by amino groups in R-CDs can promote the high yield of radiation recombination and enhance the radiation transition, thus improving the luminescence efficiency [37,38].

As can be seen from full scan XPS spectra in Fig. 3 a, B-CDs and G-CDs only contain C and O elements, while R-CDs consist of C, O and N elements. The relative contents of these elements are listed in Table S4. From B-CDs to R-CDs, the contents of C elements are increased, while the contents of O elements are gradually decreased, and R-CDs contain 1.11% of N elements, which will have an effect on the optical properties. The fine scan XPS spectra are investigated to further explore the chemical bonds of three CDs. The C 1s band of B-CDs and G-CDs (Fig. 3b) is fitted well with three dominant peaks centered at 284.7, 286.2, and 288.0 eV, corresponding to three bonding states such as CC/CC, CO, and CO bonds [39], respectively. There is a red shift of the emission peak from B-CDs to G-CDs. This red shift is accompanied by a more CO bonds and a lower CO bonds (Table S5). This phenomenon is due to the aldol condensation reaction between glucose and p-phthalaldehyde. The C 1s spectrum of R-CDs shows four component peaks, assigned to CC/CC (284.8 eV), CN (285.2 eV), CO (286.4 eV), and CO (288.6 eV) bonds, which indicates the successful incorporation of amino groups into R-CDs. The O 1s spectra for three types of CDs (Fig. 3c) display two peaks at 532.2 and 535.8 eV, corresponding to the CO and CO bonds, respectively [40,41]. The increased contents of CO bonds lead to the redshift of emission from B-CDs to G-CDs (Table S6). According to the reports, the fluorescence can be induced by the surface defects of CDs, and the surface state controls the photoluminescence property [42]. The surface of G-CDs contains more hydroxyl groups than other CDs which produce more surface defects, resulting in the highest emission intensity (Fig. 2c). Two signals of CN (399.0 eV) and NH (399.6 eV) bonds are obtained by fitting the N 1s spectrum of R-CDs [43]. The successful introduction of amino groups indicates that diethylamino groups react with hydroxyl and aldehyde groups, resulting in the red light emitting of R-CDs. The XPS results are in accordance with the FTIR spectra, which demonstrates that B-CDs and G-CDs own high contents of oxygen-containing functional groups, while R-CDs include oxygen and nitrogen-related groups. G-CDs contain more hydroxyl groups on the surface, thus increasing the green emission with the highest intensity. The luminescence center of R-CDs is attributed to amino nitrogen groups.

Fig. 4 gives a possible band structure to illustrate the luminescence process of multicolor fluorescent CDs. The luminescence center of B-CDs and G-CDs is the oxygen-containing groups related to the CO bonds. For B-CDs, after the electrons in the ground state are relaxed to a lower excited state, the excited electrons jump back from the surface oxidation state associated with the hydroxyl group to the ground state, thus emitting blue light with an energy gap of about 2.7 eV. The energy gap of HOMO (Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital) and LUMO (Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital) levels is caused by the π-π* transition of the carbon core. Due to the reaction between p-phthalaldehyde and glucose, the content of CO bonds is gradually increased for G-CDs, and a large number of local electronic states related to hydroxyl groups are formed below π* states to create new energy levels [44], thus promoting more effective radiative recombination of electrons and holes, so that the O-state is shifted and the energy level gap is reduced to 2.4 eV [45]. After the introduction of m-diethylaminophenol in the reaction process, the hydroxyl and aldehyde groups react with diethylamine groups, making amino N become the luminescence center of R-CDs. Upon electronic excitation, the charge is mainly transferred from the amino group of the electron-donating group to the carbon core, promoting a more efficient radiative complexation of the surface-confined electrons and holes, which leads to a reduction of the energy gap (2.1 eV) and the red shift of photoluminescence [46,47].

Dopamine (DA) is a significant catecholamine neurotransmitter in human body and a precursor of norepinephrine or epinephrine biosynthesis in the body, which has an important regulatory effect on the nervous center, cardiovascular, kidney, hormone secretion system and mental activities of the central nervous system. The pharmacological action of DA is dose-dependent. In different dose ranges, DA can activate dopamine receptors and sympathetic nervous system receptors to produce different peripheral vascular effects [48,49]. Therefore, detecting DA is an important and valuable work. Due to the poor biological penetration of blue-green light emitting CDs, their practical application capabilities in light sensing and biological imaging may be severely limited. To effectively improve the detection efficiency, R-CDs are selected with low fluorescence interference and good biological penetration. For the purpose of estimating the fluorescent characteristics of R-CDs, the luminescence stability is explored by corresponding experiments. The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs shows little variation with different concentrations of NaCl, pH values, UV irradiation times, and various temperatures, demonstrating that R-CDs own excellent stability (Fig. S6). For the purpose of investigating the specific quenching behavior of R-CDs to DA, 800 μM of DA, ascorbic acid, glucose, urea, glutathione, alanine, serine, L-cysteine, L-asparagine, and other biomolecules and metal ions are added, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 a, DA has an obvious quenching effect on the fluorescence of R-CDs, but other biomolecules and metal ions make no impression on the luminescence intensity of R-CDs, which means that R-CDs have high selectivity for DA and can be served as good fluorescence probes for detecting DA. It has been reported that under alkaline conditions, DA can be oxidized to dopamine quinone as an electron acceptor of quantum dots, which is beneficial to interact with the hydroxyl and amino groups of CDs [50,51]. In addition, we also investigated the selectivity of B-CDs and G-CDs towards DA and found that they have similar quenching effects as R-CDs (Fig. S6). Therefore, it can be inferred that dopamine quinone interacts with the hydroxyl groups of CDs. Additionally, the luminescence intensity of R-CDs is measured at different concentrations of DA. The luminescence intensity of R-CDs is gradually reduced with the increased concentration of DA (Fig. 5b). In the range of 0–140 μM, (F0-F)/F0 of R-CDs and [DA] own a good linear relationship in the inset curves via the following equation:
(3)
F 0
- F
= 0.00411
C
DA
+ 0.04466 ,
R
2
= 0.987
where F0 and F are the luminescence intensities of R-CDs without and with addition of DA, respectively. The minimum detection limit of R-CDs for DA is to be 0.49 μM. We also measured the response of various concentrations of tablets to R-CDs fluorescence (Fig. S8). The luminescence quenching mechanism of DA on R-CDs was also researched. In Fig. 5c and Table S7, with the increased concentration of DA, the lifetime of R-CDs is slightly changed within the range of 2.07–4.04 ns, indicating that the change of DA concentration does not alter the fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs, which is reasonable to speculate that static quenching is the quenching mechanism [52,53]. Moreover, the Stern-Volmer equation is given as follows:
(4)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
[ C ]
where F0 and F are the luminescence intensities of R-CDs in the absence and presence of DA, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [C] is the DA concentration. The quenching mechanism generally depends on temperature-induced system changes. For static quenching, the stability holds a dominant position in the system due to the combination of quenching agents and fluorescent groups to produce new substances that do not emit fluorescence. As the temperature increases, the quenching constant decreases. Fig. 5d shows the luminescence intensity of DA-R-CDs under various temperatures (293, 298 and 303 K). When the temperature increases from 293 K to 303 K, the KSV reduces from 1.396 × 104 M−1 to 7.82 × 103 M−1, conforming to the static quenching mechanism. Therefore, it can be inferred that the electrons on ground state are provocated into the excited state and left holes on the ground state orbital, forming electron hole pairs during the quenching process. DA is oxidized to dopamine quinone which can accept electrons from photoexcited CDs and interact with the hydroxyl groups of CDs, thus preventing the interaction between electrons and holes, which results in the fluorescence quenching [54]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence recovery of DA-R-CDs by AA was also discussed. As shown in Fig. S9, AA can significantly restore the fluorescence of DA-R-CDs rather than other reagents (glucose, threonine, tryptophan, etc.). As the concentration of AA increases, the quenched luminescence of R-CDs is recovered, and the same trend is observed in the optimum excitation peak (Fig. 6 ). According to the report, DA is easily combined with compounds linked carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [55]. AA plays multiple important functions in the human body. AA contains hydroxyl groups that interact strongly with DA. Fig. 6c shows that the intensity of the absorption peak is gradually recovered with the increase of AA content, suggesting that AA with strong reducibility can separate DA from DA-R-CDs through a competitive reaction with dopamine quinone redox. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetimes of R-CDs (Fig. 6d, Table S8) remain almost constant with the addition of AA, in accordance with the lifetimes of DA quenched R-CDs. The reason of fluorescence intensity recovery can be confirmed from this aspect. The luminescence of R-CDs can be restored while the addition of AA is about 10 mg/mL.

To confirm the viability of the proposed method using actual samples, R-CDs were used to detect DA in levodopa tablets. Tablets were ground and dissolved in water, DA solutions of different concentrations were prepared with tablet solutions, and R-CDs were added to these solutions to measure their fluorescence intensity. Table 2 illustrates that the average recovery rate falls between 95.00% and 101.67%. The results indicate that R-CDs have good stability and anti-interference properties. Therefore, R-CDs have been developed as a fluorescence probe to detect DA, and can be also used to the determination of DA in real samples.

In summary, multicolor CDs were successfully synthesized using chemical method at room temperature and the luminescence mechanism of tricolor CDs was systematically investigated. The luminescence center of B-CDs and G-CDs is the oxygen-containing groups related to the CO bonds. The energy gap of HOMO and LUMO levels is caused by the π-π* transition of the carbon core. As the content of CO bonds increases, new energy levels are generated below the π* states, which leads to the red shift of the emission peak from B-CDs to G-CDs. With introduction of m-diethylaminophenol, the red emission state of 598 nm is achieved due to the formation of amino N groups on the R-CDs surface. In addition, red emitting R-CDs exhibit a specific luminescence turn-off response to DA and AA can restore its fluorescence, so R-CDs serve as a fluorescent reversible switching probe for DA. This work provides a green and facile method to synthesize multicolor CDs and designs a fluorescent reversible switching probe for dopamine detection based on R-CDs.

Xiaotong Wang: Validation. Qingling Zhao: Methodology, Resources. Qinghong Song: Methodology, Resources. He Bu: Methodology, Software. Jie Gao: Investigation, Software. Lanlan Li: Software, Validation. Xiaofei Yu: Software, Validation. Xiaojing Yang: Validation. Zunming Lu: Visualization. Xinghua Zhang: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52271210, 22179032, 52171176), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. E2021202022, B2021202011), and Tianjin Health Research Project (No. ZC20112).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.123952.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots in bioimaging, biosensing and therapeutics: a comprehensive review
